content.text: Dev-Staging-Prod Environment Parity for Claude Apps
Environment parity means that dev, staging, and production behave

for everything that affects application correctness — model version, prompt content, and runtime behaviour. The environments differ only in scale, data, and API key tier. Without parity, a bug can hide in staging because the environment configuration is different from production.
If staging uses a different model or different prompts than production, staging tests are fiction. Build and test with the exact configuration that will run in production.

This sub-subtopic covers the parity requirements for Claude applications specifically, the permitted differences between environments, how to manage API keys per environment without breaking parity, and the common parity violations that cause 'works in staging, broken in prod' incidents.

attrs.alt: Dev, staging, and production environment parity for Claude apps

content.text: Parity must hold across three axes. Violating any one can mask bugs:
Model behaviour is what we test; a different model invalidates all evals
Prompt change = behaviour change; staging must test the exact prompts going to prod
Different code = different behaviour; only the image digest guarantees identity
Scale affects concurrency bugs and rate-limit behaviour
Real-data sensitivity stays in prod; staging data must have same shape for realistic tests
Dev can use a low-rate key; staging must match prod rate limits for load testing

The safest way to enforce parity is to make everything that must be identical part of the immutable image (model version, prompts, app code) and make everything that may differ a runtime environment variable injected by the orchestration layer.

# src/config.py — runtime configuration, injected per environment
import os

# These are IDENTICAL across all environments — baked into the image
MODEL_VERSION = os.environ["MODEL_VERSION"]       # from Dockerfile ARG
PROMPT_HASH = os.environ["PROMPT_HASH"]           # from Dockerfile ARG

# These DIFFER per environment — injected at deploy time
APP_ENV = os.environ.get("APP_ENV", "dev")                 # dev | staging | prod
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = os.environ["ANTHROPIC_API_KEY"]        # env-specific secret
MAX_TOKENS = int(os.environ.get("MAX_TOKENS", "1024"))
LOG_LEVEL = os.environ.get("LOG_LEVEL", "INFO")

# These differ but must remain within a valid range for all envs
REPLICA_COUNT = int(os.environ.get("REPLICA_COUNT", "1"))  # set by Helm/k8s

def validate():
    """Fail fast if any required config is missing or obviously wrong."""
    assert MODEL_VERSION, "MODEL_VERSION must be set (baked in at build)"
    assert ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY must be set"
    assert APP_ENV in ("dev", "staging", "prod"), f"Unknown APP_ENV: {APP_ENV}"
Kubernetes ConfigMap and Secret per environment

# k8s/dev/configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: claude-app-config
  namespace: dev
data:
  APP_ENV: "dev"
  LOG_LEVEL: "DEBUG"
  MAX_TOKENS: "512"           # shorter in dev to reduce cost

---
# k8s/staging/configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: claude-app-config
  namespace: staging
data:
  APP_ENV: "staging"
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
  MAX_TOKENS: "1024"          # matches prod

---
# k8s/prod/configmap.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: claude-app-config
  namespace: prod
data:
  APP_ENV: "prod"
  LOG_LEVEL: "WARNING"
  MAX_TOKENS: "1024"

A single API key used across dev, staging, and production creates two problems: a dev engineer's experiment can exhaust the production rate limit, and a production key leak would expose dev and staging too. Use separate keys per environment and separate tier levels:
Prevent cost overrun from automated tests
Match prod rate limits so load tests are realistic
Most 'works in staging, broken in prod' incidents trace to one of these violations:
in production. The snapshot alias changes silently when Anthropic releases an update, so staging starts testing the new model before production is ready.
Always use the date-stamped snapshot alias in all environments. Update all environments simultaneously when upgrading to a new snapshot.

Reading prompts from a config map or database at startup means staging can serve different prompts than production if the config map gets out of sync. Baking prompts into the image eliminates this class of violation entirely.
in staging to reduce cost causes staging tests to pass with truncated responses, while production generates full responses that may trigger different downstream parsing or display bugs.
Set MAX_TOKENS identically in staging and production
If cost is a concern, reduce the number of eval cases run rather than truncating responses
Dev can use a lower MAX_TOKENS since it is only used for quick integration smoke tests

If staging uses a dev-tier API key with very low rate limits, load tests pass trivially — the test load never approaches the real production ceiling. Use a production-tier key in staging specifically so rate-limit and concurrency behaviour matches production.

Production data cannot live in staging (PII, compliance), but staging data must have the same schema, field distributions, and edge cases that production data has. Synthetic or anonymised staging data that is too clean causes tests to pass that would fail on real production data:
Parity Verification in the Promotion Pipeline
Add a parity check to the staging-to-production gate so the pipeline fails if environments have drifted:

# .github/workflows/promote-to-prod.yml (excerpt)
  verify-parity:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [gate-3-full-validation]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Verify staging matches CI manifest
        run: |
          STAGING_DIGEST=$(kubectl get deployment claude-app -n staging             -o jsonpath="{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}")
          MANIFEST_DIGEST=$(jq -r .image_digest manifest.json)
          if [ "$STAGING_DIGEST" != "$MANIFEST_DIGEST" ]; then
            echo "PARITY VIOLATION: staging running $STAGING_DIGEST, manifest is $MANIFEST_DIGEST"
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Staging matches manifest: $STAGING_DIGEST"
Drift Detection and Scheduled Parity Checks

Environment configurations drift over time even with good automation. A Kubernetes admin may update an image tag manually; an environment variable may be patched in place. Schedule a nightly parity check that alerts on any deviation:

# .github/workflows/nightly_parity_check.yml
name: Nightly Environment Parity Check
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 3 * * *"   # 3 AM UTC nightly

jobs:
  check-parity:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run parity verification
        env:
          KUBECONFIG_DEV: ${{ secrets.DEV_KUBECONFIG }}
          KUBECONFIG_STAGING: ${{ secrets.STAGING_KUBECONFIG }}
          KUBECONFIG_PROD: ${{ secrets.PROD_KUBECONFIG }}
        run: python3 scripts/verify_parity.py
      - name: Alert on drift
        if: failure()
        run: |
          echo "Environment parity check failed — images or model versions have drifted."
          echo "Check kubectl describe deployment claude-app -n <namespace> for details."
          # In production: send to Slack / PagerDuty here
          exit 1
Add the parity check to the promotion workflow as well (as shown in the previous section) so drift between build and deploy is always caught before it reaches production.
Parity must hold for model version, prompt content, and application code — these are made identical by deploying the same immutable image to all environments
Permitted differences: scale (dev=1 replica, staging=2, prod=3–20 autoscaled), data (dev/staging use anonymised fixtures), API key tier (dev uses lower-tier; staging and prod use production-tier)
Never use mutable model aliases (claude-haiku) in any environment — always use the date-stamped snapshot alias so all environments test the same model
Prompts must be baked into the image, not injected from a config store, to prevent staging from serving different prompts than production
MAX_TOKENS must match between staging and production; reduce eval case count to manage cost, not response truncation
Staging must use a production-tier API key so load tests encounter realistic rate-limit behaviour
Add a parity verification step to the promotion gate: compare the staging-running image digest to the manifest digest before promoting to production
